query: PostgreSQL analytics tools ecosystem ClickHouse TimescaleDB 2024
answer: ClickHouse excels in high-speed analytical queries, while TimescaleDB integrates time-series data with traditional relational tables effectively. Choose ClickHouse for raw OLAP speed; opt for TimescaleDB for SQL compatibility and relational flexibility.

results.title: ClickHouse® vs TimescaleDB: Best for real-time analytics 2026
results.snippet: Modern ClickHouse® workflows address this by combining **high-throughput initial loads**, **low-latency change data capture**, and **automatic schema synchronization** for both one-time and continuous migration scenarios. This approach minimizes risk, provides an early validation of query performance, and allows teams to phase out legacy infrastructure progressively.Because ClickHouse® can ingest and query replicated data in parallel, it becomes a **seamless analytical companion** to time-series systems during transition periods. By **including all child tables in the logical replication publication**, the migration engine ensures that every insert, update, or delete from any chunk is streamed correctly to the target ClickHouse® table.The ingestion process reconstructs these chunks into a **single continuous table** in ClickHouse®, ordered by timestamp and optimized for columnar storage.This approach retains the same time-series semantics while benefiting from ClickHouse®’s **compression and vectorized execution** for analytical workloads. * **For relational time-series integration:** Choose TimescaleDB when you combine time-series data with traditional relational tables using joins, or when your team's PostgreSQL expertise outweighs the performance benefits of ClickHouse®.

results.title: PostgreSQL vs. TimescaleDB vs. ClickHouse: 2026 Performance Guide | sanj.dev
results.snippet: This is for you if you’re a developer choosing a database for time-series or analytics workloads, a platform engineer evaluating PostgreSQL vs ClickHouse, a data engineer building monitoring or IoT systems, or anyone trying to pick the right database for their specific use case. By the end of this, you’ll know the key differences between PostgreSQL, TimescaleDB, and ClickHouse for time-series, which database fits your specific workload (ingestion rate, query patterns, scale), the real performance trade-offs based on production benchmarks, and which database to choose based on your requirements. **Performance:** On the same 16-core server, ClickHouse 25.12 handles **2-3M+ data points per second** for batch inserts ClickHouse Performance Benchmarks, with query performance that’s often 10-100x faster than PostgreSQL or TimescaleDB for analytical workloads ClickHouse Performance. **The pattern:** Real-time monitoring and alerting use TimescaleDB (or InfluxDB), historical analytics use ClickHouse, and operational data stays in PostgreSQL. **Real-World Stack:** A typical analytics platform uses **TimescaleDB** for real-time metrics capture, **ClickHouse** for historical analytics, and **PostgreSQL** for operational data.

results.title: Why I chose Postgres (TimescaleDB) over ClickHouse for storing 10M+ logs - DEV Community
results.snippet: Cover image for Why I chose Postgres (TimescaleDB) over ClickHouse for storing 10M+ logs. # Why I chose Postgres (TimescaleDB) over ClickHouse for storing 10M+ logs. It's incredibly fast, compresses data like magic, and can handle petabytes of logs without breaking a sweat. I built LogTide because I needed a self-hosted log management tool that fits on a $5 DigitalOcean droplet. If I want to find all error logs from a specific service in the last hour, I don't need to learn a new query language (like LogQL or Splunk SPL). With one command, TimescaleDB automatically splits my huge logs table into smaller chunks based on time. If you're ingesting 1 billion logs a day, please use ClickHouse (or Datadog). Let me know in the comments: Would you trust Postgres with your logs, or is ClickHouse the only way? DEV Community — A space to discuss and keep up software development and manage your software career.

results.title: The Best Time-Series Databases Compared | Tiger Data
results.snippet: PostgreSQL-based solutions like TimescaleDB extend a relational database with TSDB capabilities (hypertables, compression, continuous aggregates), giving you time-series performance without abandoning SQL and the PostgreSQL ecosystem. | Tiger Data | PostgreSQL-native analytics + time-series | Full SQL (PostgreSQL) | Vertical; writes scale-up, reads scale-out on Tiger Cloud | Yes (Apache 2.0 (core) / TSL (Community Edition) | Yes (Tiger Cloud) | #4 (5.42) |. **Strengths**: Full PostgreSQL compatibility (the entire ecosystem of extensions, drivers, tooling), continuous aggregates for pre-computed rollups, columnstore compression achieving 90%+, JOINs between time-series and relational data, managed cloud offering (Tiger Cloud). **Best for**: Teams that already use PostgreSQL, workloads that need fast analytical queries combined with relational flexibility, environments where SQL is a requirement, and use cases that need to JOIN time-series data with business data. **If you need fast analytical queries on time-series data**: TimescaleDB (continuous aggregates and columnstore for pre-computed, compressed analytics) or ClickHouse (raw OLAP speed, especially when time-series is one of several workloads).

results.title: ClickHouse vs TimescaleDB: Which to Choose for Time-Series Data
results.snippet: ClickHouse and TimescaleDB are both excellent choices, but they have fundamentally different architectures and excel in different scenarios.
